data.title: Nova: A Strong Year, But Concerns Rise As Growth Slows
data.description:
In February of this year I concluded that Nova (NVMI) was a new star in the making. Following impressive sales growth and margin expansion, the company was on track to see a strong 2025, although I was a bit cautious given the prevailing valuation as well. To shed some perspective, just a $2 stock in the early 2000s and just a $20 stock in 2019, traded around the $270 mark early this year.

The company delivered on sequential revenue growth during the year, but the increases kept on getting smaller, with sequential revenue growth rapidly slowing down, a trend that makes me concerned with growth slowing down a lot, making me cautious at current levels, despite a strong track record.

Process Insights For A World Of Exciting Digital Innovations

Nova is both an innovator and provider of metrology solutions, used in advanced process control throughout the semiconductor fabrication lifecycle.

The company provides such performance solutions for process control, with the portfolio including both hardware and software, to provide further developments in the semiconductor industry. These solutions are based on optical, material, and chemical solutions, all designed to improve performance, enhance product yield, and accelerate time to market.

Actual product solutions include metrology platforms for dimensional, material, film, and chemical metrology measurements, with such solutions provided to logic, foundry, and memory solutions all over the world, with the company providing a key role on the background to advance semiconductor advancements as well.

Advancing

Needless to say, Nova has made huge strives over the past decade, this being just a $150 million business a decade ago. When I looked at the shares early in the year, the company was enjoying a real return of operating momentum in the business, with third quarter sales up 39% to $179 million, and sequential revenue growth seen in the final quarter.

The company furthermore announced a $60 million bolt-on deal to acquire German-based Sentronics, a wafer metrology tools provider for the backend of the semiconductor manufacturing. In February, Nova reported a 45% increase in fourth-quarter sales to $195 million. Operating profits came in at $55 million, for potential net earnings of around $44 million at a run rate of $5.50 per share.

That excludes interest income on net cash holdings, which were substantial at around three-quarters of a billion, even after the Sentronics deal, being equal to $23 per share.

The company guided for first quarter sales to advance to a midpoint of $210 million, with earnings likely seen around $6-$7 per share here, which reveals about a 35-40 times earnings multiple based on the earnings power. This made me a bit cautious, given the absolute valuation, reliance on Asia, and momentum seen in the shares already.

Delivering

Shares quickly ended up falling about a hundred dollars to the $170 mark in the spring, in between concerns about growth and tariffs. Shares have subsequently seen a huge boom to the $360 mark in the fall, falling to the $270 mark in November, now having recovered to the $330 mark.

This volatility, but generally positive results, has been driven by strong operating performance, as first quarter sales rose by 50% to $213 million, with strong margins displayed upon. Moreover, momentum was seen continuing at elevated levels, with second-quarter sales seen around $215 million.

Over the summer, second quarter sales rose by 40% to $220 million, with adjusted earnings of $2.20 per share reported, up two pennies compared to the first quarter. The company guided for third-quarter sales at a midpoint of $221 million, but in the end revenues were reported just shy of $225 million, with these sales up ¨just¨ 25% on the year.

By now, revenues trend around $900 million per annum, as third-quarter adjusted earnings actually fell a bit to $2.16 per share. GAAP earnings came in at $1.90 per share, with most of the reconciliation due to stock-based compensation expenses, making that earnings of $2.00 per share look fair as a realistic earnings estimate. This, however, includes about $9 million in net interest income, which is equal to about $0.28 per share pre-tax. Adjusted for all this, realistic earnings are seen around $7 per share, in line with my expectations.

The company now has $1.6 billion in gross cash and market valuation, with the business having boosted these gross holdings on the back of $820 million in convertible notes outstanding, issued earlier this year. Net cash holdings are pretty stable at $800 million. This makes that operating asset are valued around $300 per share, given a current share price near the $330 mark. Needless to say, with realistic earnings estimated around $7 per share, earnings multiples are seen in the low forties.

And Now?

After a nearly 40% plunge at the start of the year, shares doubled in the first half of the year, and after some volatility was seen during the fall; it is easy to be cautious here. By now, shares have risen about 20% since February, on a net basis, amidst a decent performance, but nothing too spectacular.

Sequential revenue improvements have been modest, with year-over-year growth rapidly slowing down, as fourth quarter sales are actually seen down a bit to a midpoint of $220 million. Adjusted earnings are seen down to a midpoint of $2.09 per share, as both metrics suggest declines, but given the conservative guidance practices in the past, the operating performance likely is fairly flattish on a sequential basis.

In between all this, I am erring a bit on the cautious side here, as valuations have been full, following a resilient stock price performance, while the operating momentum seems to fade amidst demanding valuations here. This makes me cautious here, although Nova's strong rise over the past decade and sound positioning make it very interesting to keep an eye on, yet I have no interest at current levels.

data.title: Zscaler: A Buyable Dip As Rule Of 40 Expands
data.description:
As we approach the final few weeks of 2025, the S&P 500 continues to sit near all-time highs. However, much of these gains are tied up in a concentrated group of large-cap tech stocks that are directly enmeshed in the AI trade. Many other growth stocks, meanwhile, are well off recent highs, which has created an opportunity for us to rotate more of our portfolios into these rebound plays.

Zscaler (ZS) is, surprisingly, in a technical bear market, as the stock is now down nearly 30% from highs above $320 notched in early November. Shares have sold off post-earnings, with bears now pointing to growing competitive pressures from similarly large cybersecurity peers like Palo Alto Networks (PANW), which has recently gone on a buying spree to encroach into new markets.

We're not exactly shedding tears for Zscaler, which remains up ~30% on the year. But we also should take a step back and recognize that Zscaler occupies a rarefied perch in terms of growth/profitability balance in the software industry, and this dip is a great time to refresh ourselves on the bull case in this name.

I last wrote a "Buy" article on Zscaler in October, when the stock was trading slightly above $300. Since then, Zscaler has seen a ~20% correction, making its valuation multiples more appealing. I do agree that the stock was (and remains) sitting at a valuation premium that needed some valuation. However, in today's expensive stock market, Zscaler's multiples no longer stand out, especially after its recent fall, and given the company's persistently excellent growth metrics, I'm reiterating my "Buy" rating on this name.

Since valuation is a top concern for Zscaler, let's start with a discussion of the company's latest multiples against its latest expectations. At current share prices near $240, Zscaler trades at a market cap of $38.35 billion. Netting off the $3.32 billion of cash and $1.70 billion of convertible debt from Zscaler's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $36.73 billion.

For the remainder of FY26 (which ends in July 2026), Zscaler has boosted its full-year revenue guidance to $3.28-$3.30 billion, or 22.8%-23.5% y/y growth, essentially one point stronger than its previous outlook. The company continues to expect a 22% operating margin and a ~26% FCF margin for the year, which implies $874 million in FCF.

For next year FY27, Wall Street analysts are expecting Zscaler to hit $3.95 billion in revenue (+20% y/y). If we assume Zscaler can achieve ~1 point of further margin expansion (in line with current y/y margin improvement trends) to a ~27.5% FCF margin next year, FCF would be $1.09 billion. This positions Zscaler's valuation multiples at:

  • 11.2x EV/FY26 revenue and 9.3x EV/FY27 revenue
  • 42x EV/FY26 FCF and 33.8x EV/FY27 FCF

No, Zscaler is not a value stock. At the same time, this is a company that cleanly hits the Rule of 40 every quarter, while enjoying massive new expansion opportunities in AI security and agentic IT ops (which has already hit $400 million in ARR for the company). We note as well that when we stack up Zscaler against other cybersecurity peers (all of which have seen a surge in investor interest as well as revenue growth as companies investing in data center buildouts have also upped their security stack), Zscaler is hardly a premium stock, with direct competitors like NetSkope trading substantially richer.

In my view, the core upside catalyst that AI drives for Zscaler is the fact that it's forcing many companies to rethink their IT infrastructure, and accelerating the move away from owned data centers and into the cloud to run AI applications. This should, in my view, push more demand away from Palo Alto Networks' traditional on-prem firewalls and into cloud-based security products like Zscaler. In short, Zscaler is still in the early innings of capturing a substantial shift in spending, despite recent stock weakness.

Take advantage of the dip here as a buying opportunity.

Q1 download

Let's now go through Zscaler's latest fiscal Q1 (October quarter) results in greater detail. The Q1 earnings summary is shown below:

Zscaler's revenue grew 26% y/y to $788.1 million, crushing Wall Street's expectations of $773.8 million (+23% y/y) by a three-point margin. Also impressive to note is the fact that Zscaler saw its growth rates accelerate by 5 points sequentially relative to just 21% y/y growth in Q4 (the July quarter), and 23% y/y in the preceding April.

This is an important point to make, as many software companies have touted AI as being a huge sea change for their businesses without showcasing any actual improvement in growth rates to back up those claims. That's not the case for Zscaler, which has released new security products for AI applications and new threat detection tools for agentic AI operations.

Revenue acceleration here showcases the benefit that AI is providing to Zscaler's growth rates, as does the company's pickup of customers. As shown in the chart below, the company now has 3,754 customers who generate over >$100k in ARR, adding 260 such customers within the quarter (far more than the 137 added in Q4). Meanwhile, the number of customers generating over $1 million in ARR grew 20% y/y to 698. With AI, Zscaler now has a broader set of products to cross-sell to its highest tier of enterprise customers.

Meanwhile, the company also added $189 million of net-new ARR in the quarter to end at $3.20 billion of ARR, up 25% y/y. We note that Zscaler's ARR today already covers 81% of consensus' $3.95 billion revenue expectations for FY27, leaving ample room for upside in estimates as Zscaler lands large new deals not currently reflected in ARR.

Further adding fuel for future growth is the fact that Zscaler's total remaining performance obligations, or essentially backlog, grew 35% y/y to $5.9 billion. Meanwhile, current RPO (which is the portion of backlog that will be recognized as revenue within the next 12 months) grew at a faster 47% y/y clip. All of these are strong indicators that customers are making long-term, large commitments to Zscaler, and that revenue growth isn't at risk of near-term deceleration.

On the profitability front, we note that new products are having the impact of dampening Zscaler's gross margins, which fell 1 point year/y to a still-rich 80% (yet we note that as new AI products scale, we should see tailwinds in margin as the company benefits from economies of scale on public cloud usage).

Despite this, Zscaler's controlled opex growth still allowed 1 point of y/y operating margin expansion to 22%, comfortably positioning the company's "Rule of 40" score at 48. Zscaler's own reported "Rule of 40" calculation uses free cash flow margins instead of pro forma operating margins (which is more common among enterprise software peers); with FCF margins, the company's Rule of 40 score rises to a tremendous 78 for Q1.

Key takeaways

Despite recent pessimism and concerns that Zscaler will lose its competitive luster as IT focus turns to AI, Zscaler's recent results and its acceleration in revenue growth atop enormous ARR/RPO expansion showcase that these fears are overblown. Though Zscaler did certainly have a valuation premium to burn off, as usual the selloff has likely gone too far, with security peers like NetSkope and CrowdStrike now commanding substantially richer valuation multiples. "Buy" the dip here with confidence.

data.title: Broadcom Q4 Earnings: The Price Of Ownership Includes Volatility
data.description:
With another strong quarter in the books, Broadcom (AVGO) is seeing the sort of post-earnings volatility that is common with fast-growing, high-multiple, popular theme stocks, and with Broadcom now among the clear leaders in AI silicon, it fits all those categories.

AI silicon has been transformative for Broadcom since my last article, not only propelling the shares over 320% (beating NVIDIA (NVDA), AMD (AMD), Marvell (MRVL), and the broader semiconductor industry), but also tripling the revenue base since FY’22 and significantly expanding not only the company’s served addressable markets, but its customer roster as well.

Valuation is an obvious concern with a roughly 40x forward P/E, but I’m also concerned about what it will take for Broadcom to continue to impress or surprise the Street. It wasn’t that long ago that I’d write about Broadcom’s efforts in custom AI silicon and some readers would scoff at the idea that it would be a real business; now there are articles suggesting Broadcom is going to take NVIDIA’s lunch money at recess.

I still think Broadcom is quite possibly the best-run chip company in the business, and I do expect share growth in AI network and compute. I’m content to continue holding the shares, but I have to admit that the bull thesis is increasing a gussied-up version of “just trust me, bro” predicated on huge ongoing AI capex investment.

Strong Results To Close The Year

Semiconductor earnings reports are very typically more about management’s guidance for the next quarter than the reported results, but Broadcom nevertheless posted strong numbers to close out its fiscal year.

Revenue rose 28% year over year and 13% sequentially, beating expectations by about 3%. Semiconductor revenue rose almost 35% yoy and 21% qoq, with AI-related revenue up 74% and 25% (to almost 60% of semi revenue), and legacy revenue up about 2% yoy and 16% qoq. Software revenue rose 19% yoy and 2% qoq. Semis did a little better relative to sell-side expectations, but both beat by around 3%.

Conditions for much of the legacy semiconductor business remain challenging and lackluster. Broadband is doing well, but that’s about the only area of strength, as wireless is pretty flat and other enterprise markets are soft (though I’d expect to see more strength in storage-related demand given data center capex trends).

Gross margin improved 100bp yoy and declined 50bp qoq to 77.9%, beating by about half a point. Semiconductor margin improved modestly (to around 68%) and software margin continues at a very high level (around 93%). With AI-related gross margin likely in the low-60’s, growth here is actually a bit dilutive to margins, but that should improve over time.

Operating income rose 35% yoy and 14% qoq, with margin up 350bp yoy and 70bp qoq to 66.2%. Semi OPM came in around 59% and Software around 78%. EBITDA rose by 34% yoy and 14% qoq, and beat by 5%.

Angst Over The Guide

Given the nosebleed valuations for stocks like Broadcom and NVIDIA, it’s pretty common to see institutional investors fret and fixate on the details in the near-term guidance. To that end, while Broadcom’s backlog rose almost 50% sequentially, and the AI-related backlog beat sell-side targets ($73B vs. $69B), there were apparently “whisper numbers” of $80B or more, and there are some worries about whether AI revenue in FY’26 will hit already-lofty expectations.

Likewise, there seems to be some angst over management saying that OpenAi is unlikely to generate meaningful revenue in FY’26 and whether or not Anthropic’s second large order is more of a “one and done”, not to mention the reality that this order is likely to bring in materially lower gross margins due to it being a full system rack sale (meaning more pass-through elements that depress margin) and likely splitting some of the margin with Alphabet/Google (GOOGL).

Looking out longer term (and beyond just the quarterly guide), there are worries about whether Google will pull in more TPU design content over time, basically taking some business away from Broadcom, and bringing in MediaTek on accelerators.

I don’t suggest simply ignoring potential issues, but I wouldn’t panic. As far as the backlog/imputed AI revenue growth goes, Broadcom has a record of under-promising and offering conservative guidance, and the company has had few issues winning compute and networking business. As far as Google goes, I think it makes sense from their perspective to secure the business and have backup plans, particularly as fab capacity is becoming an emerging issue with AI silicon.

I would also note that Broadcom announced another new $1B order that wasn’t OpenAI or Anthropic. Broadcom doesn’t reveal customer names until they decide to reveal themselves, but this could be Softbank (SFTBY) or even Apple (AAPL) given the long business relationship between Broadcom and Apple (and the fact that Apple is one of the relatively few companies left with the resources to engage with Broadcom on custom silicon), but there are also rumors that Microsoft (MSFT) wants to work with the company.

Share Gains In A Fast-Growing Market

Broadcom has two strong plays on AI silicon – networking and specialized processors (aka XPUs).

The networking side is largely an outgrowth of a business where Broadcom has long been the leading player, but there’s considerable opportunity in essentially stringing together multiple processors both within a data center (scale-up) and across data centers (scale-out). Most likely this will be around a third of the business over the longer term.

The company’s new Tomahawk Ultra offers ultra-low-latency switching designed for AI and high-performance computing and is directly targeting NVIDIA’s NVLink (and can reportedly connect 4x as many chips), and the new Thor Ultra (an 800G AI Ethernet NIC) can connect hundreds of thousands of processors, offering powerful performance upgrades for data center operators.

On the compute side, Broadcom is still small relative to NVIDA, but hyperscalers are clearly interested in custom silicon and I expect XPUs to outgrow GPUs in the coming years. NVIDIA can win on time-to-market and software ecosystem (and AMD offers lower-cost merchant alternatives), but Broadcom’s custom XPUs can outperform in their designed tasks and are lower cost, though they require significant upfront design work and Broadcom can’t address the software needs.

Said differently, there is room for both and legitimate use-cases for both, and I do expect strong growth (and share gains) for Broadcom on the back of scale-up networking and custom XPU demand over the next three to five years.

The Outlook

I expect over 50% revenue growth for Broadcom next year, which is frankly a huge amount of growth for a company this large. I do expect improvements in the legacy semiconductor business (which still matters), but AI is absolutely the driver here, so ongoing investments into AI capex is critical to the story. I’m expecting growth to slow to about 25% over the next five years (which is still pretty amazing for a company this size) and 15% over the long term.

On the margin side, I do have concerns about the speed at which Broadcom can scale up margins in the AI compute space; semiconductor valuation has long been very sensitive to margins, and I don’t think that has changed (even if the multiples have expanded significantly). I expect gross margin to decline for at least a couple of years, though it should stay above 70%. I likewise expect a little pressure on operating margin in the near term, and I’m curious to see if Broadcom can push into the high-60%’s over the next five years.

Turning to adjusted cash flow, I do expect some modest near-term headwinds from those margin pressures, but over the longer term I expect FCF margins comfortably back in the 40%’s, driving around 20% annualized FCF growth.

Clearly these are robust expectations, and critically dependent upon ongoing investments into AI, but I don’t see much evidence that companies are thinking of pulling back at this point. The utility of AI may be debatable in many areas, but there are a lot of industries (like insurance, banking, et al.) where adoption is quietly accelerating. I do expect the pace of investment to slow, but given that a lot of Broadcom’s efforts are focused on increased performance relative to power consumption, that directly addresses one of the biggest ongoing challenges to further AI capex expansion (the availability of adequate power supplies).

Valuation is almost a moot topic. There’s no way I can get to an attractive fair value on discounted cash flow or margin-driven EV/revenue and EV/EBITDA without even more extreme growth assumptions and/or equally extreme cuts to my estimated discount rates.

The Bottom Line

Stocks don’t go up because they’re cheap and they don’t go down just because they’re expensive. To that end, investors who have been waiting for NVIDIA to get “cheap” have waited for a very long time and missed out on some significant gains. I’m not saying that investors should just ignore valuation, but the reality is that this is a growth-driven story and so long as the growth continues without significant margin erosion, the shares will continue to look expensive and likely still go higher.

My biggest concern today is more nuanced. As I said in the open, the Broadcom AI story has gone from one that was previously ignored, dismissed, or derided to the “base case”, with some now arguing that this is the stronger play relative to NVIDIA. With those rising standards and expectations, it’s harder to achieve standout performance that moves the shares higher. So while I’m still a shareholder and I think there’s an argument for buying/owning, it’s a more crowded trade today and a riskier story if there’s any wobble in AI investment trends.

data.title: IWMI: Small Caps, Big Dividends
data.description:
My regular readers will know that I love small-cap stocks; I think there's a lot of alpha in small caps. What there is also a lot of is volatility, and so it's only natural that NEOS ETFs, a fund manager that I have covered many times before with their suite of high-income funds, would turn to harvesting income from small caps as well.

Enter the NEOS Russell 2000 High Income ETF (BATS:IWMI), which, according to NEOS' website:

The Fund seeks to distribute high monthly income generated from investing in the constituents of the Russell 2000 index and implementing a data-driven call option strategy...

Like many covered call funds, the goal is simple: own an underlying asset you expect to appreciate, and sell call options against its upside. The outcome, while often underperforming the uncovered assets, is a fund that kicks out high income. For many income investors, this is a welcome trade-off.

In the case of IWMI, the underlying asset is the aforementioned Russell 2000 index, or the "small cap index" as it's colloquially known (although it's not so much small caps anymore!). The Russell 2000 approximates the bottom third of stocks by size in the Russell 3000, which itself captures the top 3000 stocks in the US.

This dynamic has been true of IWMI's lifespan, from its inception in June of last year until now.

However, the other side of the trade-off can't be ignored. The difference is a little staggering, considering the total returns are so close to each other. A lot of the returns from IWMI come from this high distribution rate.

IWMI Overview

Here are the basics of the fund, for context:

Price $50.60
Inception 6/25/2024
Distribution Rate 14%
Dividend TTM $6.85
Dividend Frequency Monthly
19a-1 RoC % ~95%
ER 0.68%*
AUM $417.23M

*To note, while I am not a fee-sensitive investor (so long as it's justified), those who are should note that the 0.68% ER is temporarily lowered due to a fee waiver. After September 30th, 2026, the fee will rise to 0.78%.

IWMI Holdings & Strategy

The holdings are very straightforward, since the strategy is very straightforward. If your strategy is "buy the Russell 2000 and sell options against it," you really only need two holdings: the Russell 2000 long position and the short options position. That's precisely what we see with IWMI.

The options are split into two positions as they have different strikes -- a balancing act by management between upside participation and income -- but otherwise, the fund is really just a wrapper for the Vanguard Russell 2000 ETF (VTWO) and an options overlay tied to the Russell 2000 index (RTY) itself.

While some may feel that the fund managers should be replicating the index to save on fees, I see VTWO's 0.07% ER fee as reasonable to keep the fund's operations tight and ensure that the IWMI fee itself remains low.

As expected, the fund owns firms from the United States by and large, with very few headquartered outside the United States (less than 10% of firms by weight). All of these firms do the majority of the business in the US, as mandated by the index's rules.

As alluded to earlier, the "small-cap index" is no longer majority small caps. This is due to the Russell 2000's rules around inclusion; they focus on the bottom of the Russell 3000, which has been growing by average market cap for some time. So has the Russell 2000, and now the bottom third of the largest firms are over half mid-caps.

This isn't a bad thing, per se, just something to keep in mind when considering IWMI or any Russell 2000-based fund.

One of the things I like about using the Russell 2000 is that its exposure isn't majority technology stocks like most large-cap indexes. This provides diversity to portfolios that are otherwise fairly overweight mega cap tech stocks (e.g., the S&P 500). Instead, the Russell 2000's largest sectoral holdings include finance and healthcare firms, with tech still in the mix, of course.

I still believe that small caps are poised to pick up on a bull run, as market breadth is becoming more and more of a mainstream topic and point of concern for the average investor.

To this end, we've seen essentially only positive fund flows into IWMI for the past year. Investor interest is here, with only a few outflow weeks this year.

In the shorter term, we've been seeing a rotation into small caps since the market bottom in November, where they have outperformed their large-cap peers. Market breadth is correcting in some ways, although it will take a lot for it to fully correct back to levels seen pre-2020.

But for now, this is welcome for many small- and mid-cap investors.

IWMI Dividends & Taxes

On the note of IWMI's dividends, they are as volatile as the underlying asset. I mean that both figuratively and literally, as the dividends tend to move around month-to-month, but also because the dividends rely on the underlying volatility of the Russell 2000.

While they haven't swung too dramatically over its lifespan, there is little data to take from here. IWMI's dividends have ranged from ~$0.50 to $0.66. It seems likely that the fund will be able to maintain its distribution rate, although this may come at the expense of future upside should the Russell 2000 continue to perform well.

There is an important note here on taxes. Since IWMI uses index options inside the ETF wrapper, its distributions are considered return of capital ("RoC"). This is different from the destructive RoC that some funds kick back to investors; RoC from options-based ETFs are done because of an IRS accounting rule.

So most of the income IWMI produces falls under this designation, meaning that the vast majority of distributions to investors are classified as RoC. Investors in taxable brokerage accounts often consider RoC advantageous because it counts against one's cost basis. This means that distributions received are often not taxable in the year they are received, as the investor will choose when to sell the shares and realize the gains, despite the dividends hitting their account much earlier.

For reference, if your cost basis hits zero, any future distributions will be taxable in the year they are received, but count as long term capital gains.

According to IWMI's latest 19a-1 form, the approximation from NEOS ETFs on their RoC percentage is at 95% for the year. This may change, and will be finalized when the full tax forms come out. Given that VTWO's dividend is so low, I expect that this 95% figure is likely to accurate.

Conclusion

Small caps have been on my radar lately, especially due to their recent outperformance of large cap stocks. Enter IWMI, which promises both exposure to small caps and high income. While I am not personally investing in this fund for my income account, as I see further upside ahead for small caps and so prefer the uncovered ETF, I think that this fund will serve investors well who would like to diversify their equity income funds across market caps.

Adding in IWMI as a satellite holding alongside large cap funds like SPYI could make a lot of sense, and allow for more diversity in volatility regimes and the potential for outperformance moving forward.

For those reasons, I am giving IWMI a "Buy" rating, although with the caveat that I think uncovered small caps are a better buy here for total return investors who do not need the income from this fund.

Thanks for reading.

data.title: VYMI Over IDV: Forget The Higher Yield, It's All About Quality And Growth
data.description:
Looking ahead to 2026, as the Trump administration is impacting global markets with new trade directives, it can be difficult to look at interesting high yield opportunities outside of the US, but it is a very relevant strategy as valuation multiples in the US are stretched at very high levels. Looking at global opportunities can be a way to secure high yields with lower risks. A way to do so is with global ex US dividend ETFs, and today we will look at two large players of the industry: the iShares International Select Dividend ETF (IDV) and the Vanguard International High Dividend Yield ETF (VYMI).

While they have the same investment goal, these two ETFs are operating very different strategies. I will give a hold rating to IDV and a buy rating to VYMI. The YTD performance of IDV can seem to be impressive, but I think that is more about mean reversion than an actual premium in performance for this fund. Over the long term, the risk/reward profile of VYMI is a lot more interesting, the fund is cheaper, and exposed to growing markets such as India and Japan.

The "Value Trap" Risk

Naturally, when comparing two income funds, the first metric that we want to look at is the dividend yield. On this metric, IDV is doing significantly better with a distribution yield of 4.46% against 3.79%. This is a non-negligible 0.67% advantage in favor of IDV, and in addition this higher dividend is coming with lower valuation metrics. The fund’s P/E ratio is 9.91x and its P/B ratio is 1.05x against 11.53x and 1.43x for VYMI, which is much higher, especially for the P/B ratio. The direct conclusion is that IDV is inherently more of a deep value fund, while VYMI might be a bit more tilted towards growth themes, notably about its geographical split that we will mention below.

My problem with IDV is that the apparently cheaper portfolio is not the bargain that it appears to be. A P/B ratio of 1x is very low and indicates that the market is already pricing headwinds affecting portfolio companies. If a solid and mature company were to distribute stable and high dividends, I don’t think that its P/B would be that low. And actually, if you look at the fund’s mandate which is only to capture high dividend opportunities, there is indeed a value trap risk. IDV is tracking the Dow Jones EPAC Select Dividend Index, which is looking at high dividend companies in the EPAC region with a 3Y track record of dividends, with the actual dividend per share being higher than the last three years average, and some other metrics to ensure the profitability of selected companies. On the other hand, VYMI is tracking the FTSE All-World ex US High Dividend Yield Index with a broader geographical reach that is using a more quantitative approach that you will find detailed here rooted in forward metrics. Basically, IDV is backward looking and VYMI is forward looking. This is the key difference, and this is why the valuation multiples of VYMI can be higher: this approach will for me result in a higher quality portfolio, and the historical returns that we will see below are basically showing that the two funds are showing the same level of performance but with very different risks.

The current economic situation with still high interest rates above 4.10% (10Y) are posing a risk to companies with low valuations: they often have a high level of debt or are in a decreasing growth momentum. As IDV is focused on companies with lower valuations, the risk is that you will end up owning a portfolio made up of distressed companies.

Structural Divergence

Another key differentiating element lies in the portfolio composition. VYMI is extremely diversified with 1531 companies in its portfolio, against only 101 for IDV, which is much more concentrated. Sometimes, concentrated bets backed by a strong strategy can be a really efficient way to invest, but I don’t think that IDV is one of those funds, and it only gives you concentration risk.

The top 10 holdings of IDV are accounting for 17.93% of which 10.62% in the top 3 alone, which is significant for a global ETF meant to offer you diversification. This is not a “normal” positioning, and it should be backed with concrete results (which are lacking in this case). Benefiting from its very large portfolio, VYMI is much more diversified. The top 10 holdings are accounting for only 13.27% of the fund, of which 4.53% of the fund. This means a very low exposure to company specific risks, precisely what you want from a global ex US ETF that will invest in companies that you don’t really know.

In addition, the higher concentration of IDV is coming with a high portfolio turnover of 27%, against only 11% for VYMI. This means that the high yield approach of IDV is lacking conviction, and that indeed the quality of the portfolio companies is not good enough, and there is a need to rotate the portfolio frequently while VYMI is much more confident on its strategy. In terms of costs, this also explains why VYMI is less expensive.

The expense ratio of the fund is 0.17%, against a very expensive 0.50% for IDV. Naturally, this has a direct consequence on the popularity of both funds: the approach of VYMI results in $13.8 billion in AUM against "only" $6.5 billion for IDV. Strangely enough, if we look at the follower count of these two funds on Seeking Alpha, it appears that IDV is a lot more popular: 57.23 thousand followers for IDV against 13.48 thousand followers for VYMI. I think that the reason behind this difference is the higher dividend yield of IDV, making it more attractive to the income investor community of this website, but I will argue that income alone is not everything, and that you should reconsider IDV for VYMI.

The average daily trading volume of IDV is $31.1 million (0.48% of the fund), enough to guarantee a comfortable median bid/ask spread of 0.03%, while the average daily trading volume of VYMI is $89.4 million (0.65% of the fund), providing investors with an excellent bid/ask spread of 0.01%: one additional argument in favor of VYMI.

Old World Stagnation vs. New World Growth

In this part, I will explain the differences in the two funds’ composition using sectoral and geographical splits below:

Region & Country

VYMI

The most evident difference is the fact that IDV is more heavily centered around Europe, which is not totally surprising given that the region is home to many mature companies with high dividend yields, but this also comes with headwinds in the current economic landscape. The fund is mainly focused in the UK, Italy and Spain, and there is a risk that the commercial tensions between the European Union, the UK and the US’s protectionist trade policies lead to depressed valuations. The region is also seeing high levels of debt (we mentioned before that this is an issue due to the fund’s focus on cheap companies), and low growth metrics. Besides income, the fact that the fund is not very exposed to tech growth and demographic tailwinds with its sectoral split makes me question the relevance of this focused bet currently.

VYMI is taking a very different approach with an also strong exposure to the UK but completed by its exposure in Asia, mainly in Japan. I do think that the Japan exposure can be a good way to blend the traditional value style of the country with its newfound growth dynamic following corporate reforms, which is a tailwind absent from IDV's portfolio. In addition, while still dealing with value companies, the positioning of the fund to more dynamic countries such as China, Taiwan, and India can be a way to get a “safe” emerging markets exposure, benefiting from strong trends such as demographic growth and increase of purchasing power, while a strong bet in Italy and Germany with aging populations is causing long-term growth headwinds. This is exactly the difference highlighted in the previous part of this analysis: one ETF is backward looking and the other is forward looking.

In addition, looking at the sectoral splits, both funds are heavy with financial companies, making sense given their focus on dividends, but VYMI has a broader reach thanks to its underallocation to Consumer sectors that also allow the fund to benefit from Growth and Tech themes with what you could call a growth at a reasonable price approach, highly relevant in the current economic landscape. IDV bets more on sectors that can be seen as bond proxies (low growth and sensitivity to interest rates) such as Utilities and Non-Energy Minerals. I do think that the fact that this bet is preventing the fund from benefiting from Electronic and Health Technology is a miss: the industry is maturing and there are now great value opportunities to seize in these dynamic sectors.

As a reference you will find below both funds' top 10 holdings

As you can see, there is not a significant overlap in key holdings, and this is consistent with the fact that the to funds are taking two very different approaches.

Why the "Buy" Case for VYMI is Clear

We will finish this analysis with performance / risk metrics, and it will be clear that VYMI is a superior investment. Let’s start with total returns:

If we only look at total returns, it is actually quite hard to draw a conclusion. IDV outperformed in the short term (3Y and below), but VYMI is doing better on longer time frames. The short term outperformance of IDV this year is for me more of a phenomena of mean reversion driven by two factors: a very deep undervaluation of the portfolio (they where oversold for a long time, and now bouncing back closer to their fait value), a Cyclical "Junk Rally" where over a short period of time low quality assets can outperform quality ones in early market recoveries because investors are looking at cheap assets after seeing volatility. Now that this "easy" money is done and that the company portfolio are back closer to their historical valautions, there is no upside remaining because they lack innovation and growth: this is where we are now, and in 2026 I don't expect the outperformance of IDV to be sustained against VYMI.

Overall, as income ETFs should be considered long term investments, VYMI might be more appealing, but we need risk metrics to really tell the full story.

About risks, the first thing that I will say is that IDV, despite its positioning centered around the old economy and away from growth themes, is a lot riskier than VYMI (which can be partly attributed to the concentration & turnover duo). The standard deviations of the fund are 12.89% (3 years), 16.08% (5 years), and 16.96% (10 years) against only 11.23% (3 years) and 13.64% (5 years) for VYMI. Naturally, similar returns and lower risks are directly pointing to better risk-adjusted returns for VYMI, and this is also true for the 3Y time frame despite higher total returns for IDV. We will measure this with Sharpe ratios: 1.06 (3 years), 0.64 (5 years), and 0.46 (10 years) for IDV against 1.10 (3 years) and 0.76 (5 years) for VYMI. And if this is not enough to convince you that VYMI is less risky, I will complete it with the drawdown risk. Over the last five years, the maximum drawdown of IDV was -25.79% against -21.41% for VYMI. I think that the case is now clear.

Conclusion

To invest in the global dividend space in 2026, you have to consider other metrics than the sole dividend yield and think about both capital gain potential (because you can clearly see with total returns that with both IDV and VYMI you get more than the dividend), and the resilience of your portfolio (and here it is a clear win for VYMI).

IDV is one of these funds putting yield above everything else to the detriment of the quality of the portfolio, and this is how you end up with a severe loss when the market shifts. In addition, the fund is very expensive for nothing (directly hitting the difference in yield, in reality it is not +0.67 percentage points but 0.34), has a high portfolio turnover, and higher risks. Over the long term, I am convinced that VYMI will prove to be the best ETF.

data.title: Market Outlook 2026: A Goldilocks Vibe Forecast Through Tech And Energy
data.description:
The year in the market has been quite the ride. Big tech and AI have dominated headlines and indices. And yes, shifts are afoot in the technology landscape, which rightfully deserve their weightings in investments, funds, and portfolios. The capex spending of hyperscalers—Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Meta (META), Microsoft (MSFT) and Amazon (AMZN) — is mirroring this changing technology landscape. However, the energy sector is broadly meeting the moment of increasing energy demand owing to fundamental growth in the U.S. and around the globe.

The U.S. grid is going to have more electrons flowing through it in the future. These investments are being made now, reflected in the $1 trillion of capital spending by publicly traded utilities up to 2029. Energy demand is also global, with U.S. LNG supply increasing in the next five years, comprising a solid trajectory for natural gas. Remember, oil and gas are depletable resources that must be produced continuously, steadily, and efficiently. U.S. shale has that down to a fine art and science. And meanwhile, nuclear, renewables and other sources are added to the mix to complete the offering.

What does that mean? Generally, economic growth is increasing in the U.S., and around the globe, as economies modernize according to their pathways. The growing regions globally have been on the radar for many years now, and projections have proved out. The trade and tariff episodes are being digested. One net result has been other countries making bets in our tech and energy sectors. Actually, upward of $2 trillion has been announced by Europe, Japan, UAE, Saudi Arabia, South Korea, and others.

Are we in a bubble?

The looming question through the second half of the year was: "are we in a bubble?" From a Dec. 4 event in San Francisco, chipmaker AMD’s (AMD) CEO Lisa Su stated:

“We are not in a bubble. This is the most transformative tech of a lifetime and its early days—we haven’t (even) gotten started.”

She sees so much demand for the tech and that the cash flow is there (from her supply side position). Considering the models of 12 months ago, people are completing more complex tasks (speaking about AI use). She said they would be doing more if there was more capacity and power wasn’t a constraint. She expects the data center segment to grow considerably at AMD.

The entire AI value chain is spreading—vertically, horizontally, with many unknown knowns concerning the rate of diffusion and adoption. It's particularly expansive across model makers, hyperscalers and data center developers to utilities and energy players. Some have considered the mega deals between big tech, chipmakers and modelmakers to be circular, with tens of billions flowing between clients and customers. Most recently, Anthropic committed to purchasing $30 billion of Microsoft Azure compute capacity and to contract additional capacity up to 1 gigawatt. Anthropic is also committing to 1 gigawatt of compute capacity with Nvidia's (NVDA) Grace Blackwell and Vera Rubin systems.

AI models are advancing and differentiating themselves. At a Silicon Valley event in Dec. I attended, cofounder Daneila Amodei said that significant research goes into a model’s character and its guardrails, an area in which Anthropic displays leadership. It's why Microsoft is partnering with them for enterprise applications and ventures.

Microsoft will provide broader access to Claude’s frontier models for businesses, through Microsoft’s Azure AI Foundry. This will make Claude the only frontier LLM model available on all three of the world’s most prominent cloud services— Microsoft Azure, Google Cloud, and Amazon Web Services (AWS). Nvidia and Microsoft are committing to invest up to $10 billion and up to $5 billion, respectively, in Anthropic. These are commitments in partnership and platform development.

Modelmaker Anthropic has managed to spend less resources than competitors but also attract and forge new partnerships with the likes of Microsoft, AWS, and Nvidia. Anthropic expects revenue to scale in accordance with scaling trajectories, and "these scaling laws have held surprisingly stable" Amodei mentioned. At Wired Magazine’s event, she noted that models are still getting smarter, not plateauing at present, with revenue continuing on the same curve.

Given the hundreds of billions, possibly trillions, required to scale AI and its infrastructure in the next five years or so, consortia are necessary and happening. It's equally true on the financing side.

When attending an AI Summit at Oklahoma State University in April 2025, where big tech, AI model developers, high-level government officials, and energy players convened, the emphatic need for capacity was mentioned by Anthropic, Google, Amazon’s AWS and Meta. At the beginning of the year, the first Stargate projects had been announced shortly after Trump’s inauguration. Less than one year later, firms are finding their positions in these new developments. Part of Stargate Abilene is energized. The pace of data center and AI infrastructure development is moving at a clip. Much economic activity flows from this.

In energy, partnerships are happening to meet the challenge to power the way in which people are using technology and will in the future. Big energy players like GE Vernova (GEV) and NextEra have staked their claims in different ways too. GE Vernova is manufacturing vital equipment like gas turbines and forging select partnerships. NextEra is focused on multi-source energy infrastructure build-outs with top hyperscalers. They are also diving deeper into using AI and their digital-firm partnerships to unlock the future. Currently, NextEra (NEE) is targeting 15 GW geared toward data centers for 2035. The firm will spend capex of $74 billion to 2029 in a highly diversified manner.

[Note: This is for illustration about large load hook ups, data centers, from an article I'll pin in comments. Also see how and why capacity from crypto firm facilities is being leveraged for high-performance compute capacity.]

Competitors continue to change the landscape in technology and energy. The recent new announcement by startup Unconventional AI, with Bezos a funder, is gunning for “a novel, more energy-efficient computer for AI,” according to a Bloomberg report. The startup has a $4.5 billion valuation. A semiconductor expert and AI startup founder, Tyson Tuttle said, "new people will come," in an August interview. They absolutely are. I've been quasi-pitched by several AI startups in the last few months at events. Legacy players are retooling too and re-strategizing, such as Intel in partnership with Nvidia. I wouldn't count anyone out.

Patiently Expect the Unexpected

My outlook for the market is cautiously optimistic as it attempts to digest a new normal. To “normalize” what's happening, according to the charts, holding a composite of the market is a reasonable strategy. On Jan. 1, the S&P 500 was around 5,880 and stands at 6,870 (Dec. 11), nearly a 1,000-point increase, or roughly 16%. That’s roughly 8 times U.S. GDP growth of 2%. Info tech is roughly one-third of the S&P, dragging the market down nearly 13% in Q1 2025 and then boosting it again.

Noodling it out: I’d shoot for 7,100, with modest gains to other sectors, including info tech. That shaves down the P/E to 27 from 29 and accounts for 5% growth of EPS. In a slightly more positive case scenario and continued decent market vibes, then 7,250. However, mid-term election outcomes and any geopolitical events of a market-breaking nature could affect the mood.

The market reflects the collective belief of the value of firms’ economic activity, but there is a lot of noise too. To date, the belief and expectation that has accompanied info tech and price-to-earnings has the market at ~29, a historical high. However, top hyperscalers Amazon at 47, Apple 38, and Microsoft 34, are the outliers, compared to Nvidia, Meta and Alphabet/Google, just below the S&P average. There's a case of continued positive vibes that gets us to 8,100. My vibe says take a middling 7,600. If you add in a possible SpaceX IPO (SPACE), we could add a 200 lift to the index.

[Sourced from Kantar (Top 100 Most Valuable Global Brands) and Mendonca, Pereira, Godinho (2004). Rankings for 2020-2025 based on Kantar BrandZ valuations.]

The info tech and energy sectors, with continued capex will benefit the rest of the economy. Inflation is troubling, but the Fed will likely stay in a slightly constrictive zone ahead. Loosening interest rates adds fuel to the equity fires. Hopefully, we’ll find the happy Goldilocks sweet spot. It is hard to argue with perception though, as reflected in the world’s most valuable brands. It's not just about the number. I’ll call the Goldilocks number of 7,600, with a happier market of 7,800.

data.title: Hartford Moderate Allocation Fund Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Portfolio managers from Hartford Funds and years of experience

Vernon J. Meyer, CFA, 38 years

Allison Mortensen, CFA, 31 years

Jim Glendon, CFA, 16 years

Top Holdings (%)

11.73

7.54

7.49

7.23

7.16

5.79

3.48

2.54

Hartford Small Cap Value Fund (HTSCX)

1.67

Hartford Small Company Fund (IHSAX)

1.66

Percentage Of Portfolio

99.77

Holdings and characteristics are subject to change. Percentages may be rounded.

Market Overview

Geopolitics, trade policy, and monetary signals dominated markets during the third quarter. NATO’s warning to Russia over airspace violations kept geopolitical risk elevated, while US government shutdown threats added domestic uncertainty. Trade policy developments were mixed: new tariffs on timber and lumber and broader tariff threats contrasted with incremental progress on negotiations, leaving global supply chains and inflation expectations in focus. The Federal Reserve cut rates for the first time since late 2024, citing labor market softness, and signaled openness to further easing if conditions deteriorate. Inflation remained near 3%, reinforcing expectations for a gradual policy shift. These factors, combined with currency volatility and fluctuating energy prices, shaped investor positioning across regions and styles.

Equities led performance for the quarter, with US large caps ahead of developed international markets, while emerging markets outpaced both, benefiting from improving trade sentiment and a weaker dollar. Growth styles generally held an advantage over value, though leadership rotated as investors weighed technology optimism against cyclical recovery themes. Fixed income delivered positive returns but lagged equities, as falling Treasury yields supported core bonds and tighter credit spreads1 boosted corporate debt. High yield and emerging market debt outperformed investment-grade bonds, reflecting risk-on sentiment despite lingering macro uncertainty. Overall, the quarter highlighted the interplay between policy shifts, geopolitical developments, and sector rotation, reinforcing the importance of diversified positioning across asset classes.

Performance Summary

  • Hartford Moderate Allocation Fund (I Share) (HBAIX) underperformed its benchmark during the period.
  • The main driver of the Fund’s underperformance vs. the benchmark was performance of the underlying positions relative to their individual benchmarks. In particular, Hartford Core Equity Fund, Hartford International Opportunities Fund, and Hartford Large Cap Growth ETF underperformed their respective benchmarks.
  • Asset allocation decisions contributed positively to performance during the period, with the portfolio benefitting from exposure to US small-cap equity.

Positioning & Outlook

  • Over the period, we increased our positions in Hartford World Bond Fund and Hartford Core Equity Fund while reducing our positions in Hartford Core Bond ETF and Multifactor Developed Markets (ex-US) ETF.
  • We recognize that inflation is trending lower but remains sticky in services. Global policy easing supports risk assets, yet the margin for error is tight given high valuations and persistent macro risks, including fiscal deficits and potential rate shocks.
  • We remain constructive on equities, with US stocks regaining leadership as AI-driven earnings growth continues. However, we are mindful of elevated valuations and risks from tariffs, trade tensions, and a weakening labor market. Within international markets, we have removed our underweight position in emerging markets (EM). The outlook is improving for EM equities, which benefit from a weaker dollar, resilient global growth, and rate cuts by central banks in developed markets.
  • In fixed income, we prefer quality and short duration2, as long-term US bonds face upward rate pressure from fiscal deficits and inflation risks. We think high-quality investment-grade credit and short-term Treasuries offer resilience and attractive carry.

1 Spreads are the difference in yields between two fixed-income securities with the same maturity but originating from different investment sectors.

2 Duration is a measure of the sensitivity of an investment’s price to nominal interest-rate movement.

data.title: Techtronic Industries: Focus On Its Largest Customer
data.description:
I'm staying bullish on Techtronic Industries (TTNDY) (TTNDF) (0669.HK).

Its key client's quarterly results and investor event announcements suggest that TTNDY has a bright future.

My earlier August 7, 2025, article highlighted its above-expectations 1H25 gross profit-to-sales.

Don't Worry About Customer's Growth Deceleration

TTNDY's financial numbers are only published bi-annually. The firm's major partner offers clues about its recent performance.

Its annual report states that "the Group’s largest customer" represented "44.6%" of "total (2024) revenue."

At its FY24 briefing, TTNDY said that "our relationship with Home Depot (HD) is a major reason that we continue to flourish in that (North American) region." An analyst from Citigroup (C) also described "Home Depot" as its "dominant customer" at the call.

It's reasonable for me to infer that HD is TTNDY's top client.

HD's YoY turnover expansion slowed from 2QFY25's (YE end-Jan) +5% to 3QFY25's +3%. The "same store sales growth/SSSG" of +0.2% in the latest three-month period also fell short of the +1.3% consensus projection as per S&P Capital IQ.

But I'm still optimistic about TTNDY's near-term prospects for a number of reasons.

HD indicated at its 3QFY2025 results meeting that "underlying demand in the business remained relatively stable sequentially." It credited the underperformance to "a lack of storms relative to historic norms." This is a one-off, which has little impact on business fundamentals.

Also, Techtronic has outdone its key customer previously.

TTNDY's 1H2025 topline was 7.1% higher on YoY terms. HD registered inferior SSSG of -0.3% and +1.0% for 1QFY25 and 2QFY25, respectively. In my opinion, the disparity is attributable to a difference in sales mix.

HD revealed at a September 2025 Goldman Sachs (GS) forum that "our Home Depot business is equally split about 50-50, the Pro (Professionals) and the consumer."

TTNDY's "Power Equipment" division, which its professionals-focused brand MILWAUKEE falls under, contributed 95% of 2025 interim revenues.

HD's 3QFY25 call remarks suggested that "our pros are reporting months that they're booked out." On the other hand, it drew attention to "ongoing consumer uncertainty."

I think that Techtronic's latest performance is likely to be superior as compared to HD, given the former's relatively greater focus on the pro segment.

Separately, HD's 3rd quarter management commentary noted "big ticket comp (comparable) transactions for those over $1,000 were positive 2.3% compared to the third quarter of last year." It explained that "we continue to see customers trade up for innovation."

The above-mentioned plays into the company's strengths. At the H1 call, TTNDY stressed that "we increased our R&D spend to 4.6% of sales, 50 basis points higher" YoY to support "disruptive innovation, new product development."

TTNDY's latest disclosure reinforces my bullishness regarding its outlook.

It issued a "voluntary announcement" on Dec. 11 without providing any quantitative information. The group shared that its "point of sales (POS) demand trends remained strong across all core verticals through the month of November for both the (professionals-focused) MILWAUKEE and (consumer-focused) RYOBI brands."

HD's Investor Event Has A Favorable Read-Across For TTNDY

HD hosted its "Investor and Analyst Conference" or "Investor Day" on Dec. 10.

The most critical takeaway for me was the upward revision in its "Total Addressable Market/TAM" projection from $1.0 trillion to $1.1 trillion.

HD left its Consumer sub-segment TAM unchanged at $0.5 trillion. But it lifted the Pro estimate by +20% to $600 billion.

That's good news for TTNDY whose MILWAUKEE brand targets this category of clients.

HD's Professional Customer Mapping

As per the chart presented above, HD currently has a limited presence in the "medium/large pro" and "complex project purchase occasion" areas.

At the Investor Day, "medium/large professionals" are profiled as those who "work on complex projects" and belong to "1 of 3 groups: a medium to large renovator or remodeler, single-family custom homebuilder and single-family tract builder."

According to the investor presentation, "50% of the ($0.6 trillion) addressable market opportunity represents pros working on complex projects."

TTNDY referred to "MILWAUKEE" as the "number one professional brand" in its annual report. HD will most probably have to leverage on MILWAUKEE's popularity to penetrate the "medium/large professionals" segment.

At its '25 interim earnings call, Techtronic mentioned its "M18 AutoStop drill driver." This has been designed with an automatic shutoff function, which is activated when the user is at risk of an injury. It emphasized that the tool provides "a major advantage to any contractor that cares about safety on a job site."

You can imagine that TTNDY's "M18 AutoStop" is the kind of sophisticated product that HD needs to grab a bigger slice of the "complex projects" pie.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Variant Views

Weather conditions can be a substantial factor influencing the short-term results of both Home Depot and Techtronic. This is evidenced by HD's Q3 SSSG miss.

TTNDY could fail to align its future product development with the ambitions of its largest customer. If it prioritizes consumer-focused offerings over professional-related ones in the future, that might prompt HD to seek out alternative suppliers.

The Final Word

My "Buy" rating for the stock remains intact.

I valued "TTNDY at a fair P/E-to-ROE ratio of 1x" in the early-Aug update. I'm sticking to the same approach here. The consensus FY2026 "return on equity" forecast for Techtronic is 19.3%, implying a target P/E of 19.3x.

TTNDY's forward one-fiscal-year earnings multiple is 15.2 times now, as per S&P Capital IQ. The shares boast a potential upside of +27% (19.3/15.2).

I view its prospects for the near term and long run as promising, after analyzing HD's disclosures.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Arqit Hasn't Earned The Benefit Of The Doubt, Yet
data.description:
Arqit Quantum (ARQQ) has been a stock I’ve consistently been bearish on since early January. Well, my take on Arqit hasn’t changed, even after a double-digit pullback year to date, down over 23%. I think the hype around quantum stocks has subsided, and valuations are returning to levels where fundamentals actually justify them. In Arqit’s case, that means further downside risk in my view, both on the fundamentals and the technical setup. The stock just broke its strong support at the EMA200 at $30 and EMA21, and is trading at around $28. I see the risk-reward as unfavorable at current levels and would advise investors to watch for confirmation of a rebound.

Arqit is still trading more on hype than on tangible evidence of near-term monetization. I’m maintaining the stock with a sell, and I expect its valuation to continue to normalize lower as investor enthusiasm for the broader quantum theme fades. To keep a long story short, I don’t see a catalyst that re-rates this name upward anytime soon.

Stock performance and past coverage:

Arqit has been getting hammered, along with the broader tech sector, amid revived fears of an imminent AI bubble pop. The stock is down around 10% over the past month, holding on in line with peers, with D-Wave (QBTS) down 8.7%, Rigetti (RGTI) down 21%, Quantum Computing (QUBT) down 1.1% and IonQ (IONQ) down another 6.6%.

Still, Arqit is actually underperforming most peers year to date, down 25.4%, against QBTS up 219%, RGTI up 71.7%, QUBT down 24%, and IONQ up 23.7%.

And while that might sound like a relative buying opportunity on paper, I don’t see it that way. In my view, Arqit’s underperformance isn’t a case of the market overlooking a fundamentally strong name but a market correctly discounting a story that hasn’t delivered. Arqit simply hasn’t earned the benefit of the doubt, and until the company produces clear evidence of sustainable demand, meaningful contract wins, or a credible path to profitability, I believe the stock will continue to lag the quantum peers.

I last covered the stock in late September, when it traded at $41, maintaining my sell from late June, when it traded at $37.7. It is now down 29% and 21% from both these calls, respectively.

Arqit, from where I’m standing, remains a story stock, and earnings a few days ago on the 9th of December did little to change that.

Earnings rundown:

According to the company’s CEO, Andrew Leaver, 2025 “was a year of building momentum for the company as well, momentum in prospective customer engagements across our key markets, momentum in revenue and momentum in contracted backlog coming into fiscal year 2026. Fiscal year 2025 was also a year of broadening our product and service footprint.”

For FY2025, Arqit reported revenue of $530K, missing consensus by $10K. Nearly all of it came in the second half, with H2 revenue rising to $463K versus just $67K in H1. The company generated revenue from seven contracts, including two multi-year agreements that contribute recurring revenue, spanning telecom operators as well as government, defense, and enterprise customers.

Visibility for FY26 is more optimistic, and the company expects to “to generate revenues of approximately $1.2 million in fiscal year 2026,” according to the press release, but “Recognition of revenue under existing contracts is subject to a number of factors including, amongst others, the successful delivery of our products under the terms of those contracts, and the assumption that customers will not cancel, delay or amend the terms their contracts.”

So, again, it all depends on management’s execution and delivery. And that has been the core issue with Arqit from the start. The company talks about momentum, but the numbers continue to tell a much smaller story. The way I see it, even the FY26 guidance of $1.2 million in expected revenue is hardly enough to move the needle for a company still burning $2.5 million per month. The word momentum was mentioned 9 times during the call, and it remains to be seen whether it will materialize into meaningful, repeatable revenue.

Valuation:

Arqit is expensive at current levels. Arqit’s 69x forward sales multiple is not “cheap” by comparison to peers and remains expensive for a company producing just $0.53 million in annual revenue, burning $2.5 million per month, and guiding to only ~$1.2M next year.

Forward growth expectations also favor peers: IonQ is projected to grow 105%, QUBT 80%, and QBTS 65%, while Arqit provides no forward growth estimate. Profitability metrics paint an equally challenging picture. Arqit posts extreme negative returns, ROE at -181% and ROTC at -99.8%, and burns cash at a rate similar to peers despite generating far less revenue.

Conclusion and the risks:

I see more downside ahead and would advise investors to revisit Arqit only if the stock resets to a level that properly reflects its execution risk and lack of visibility. That said, I want to be clear that there is a challenge to my bearish view. On the upside, Arqit could benefit from renewed speculative flows into quantum stocks if sentiment turns bullish, especially if larger peers post headline-grabbing contract wins or governments re-emphasize quantum security spending. A material multi-year contract with a Tier-1 player or a government agency would also make me reconsider my rating. However, at this stage, those remain hypothetical rather than base-case assumptions. More specific to Arqit, though, what would change my view is not only incremental revenue growth or management commentary around “pipeline momentum,” but tangible proof of scale through accelerating contract wins, improving revenue visibility, and a clear reduction in cash burn relative to growth.

Until then, Arqit remains a speculative story stock in my books.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: National Beverage: Strong Margins, But A Slowing Growth Story - Hold
data.description:
Thesis

An unconventional but high-quality Company in the non alcoholic beverage sector is National Beverage Corp. (FIZZ).

The Company is not so much interested in aggressive volume, but profitability.

As i see it, the fiscal Q2 2026 financial results reaffirm the company’s track record and art of maintaining impressive margins despite facing cost pressures and stiff competition.

Fizz isn’t chasing market share via price discounting or huge promotions, but is instead investing in the power of its brands, anchored by LaCroix and other premium sparkling options.

The recent results showed stable sales with a slight increase vs. last year, but with operating efficiencies showing significant improvement.

Management remains focused on value creation through margins and cash flow and it doesn’t mind seeing growth slowing down a bit.

With a P/E of 18x, the stock already prices in much of this quality, limiting the immediate upside margin.

Financials

In Q2 2026, National Beverage recorded a mild increase in net sales on an annual basis, continuing the stabilization picture that had begun to take shape since the Q1 2026.

Revenue growth was at a low single-digit rate, significantly lower than the double-digit levels of previous years but clearly we see a better picture compared to the stagnation observed in a part of fiscal year 2025.

The gross profit margin was further strengthened, thanks to a better sales mix, reduced promotional activities and a gradual de-escalation of certain raw materials and transportation.

Compared with the same quarter last year, gross margins moved higher by several tens of basis points, confirming that the pricing strategy remains effective.

Operating expenses increased modestly, with management showing emphasis in salary costs and advertising expenses.

This resulted in operating profit growing at a faster pace than sales, improving the operating margin compared to both Q2 FY25 and Q1 FY26.

Net profit followed the same trend, with y-o-y growth and an improvement in the net profit margin.

The company showed strong FCF generation, with no significant capital needs and no net debt.

Corporate Developments, Strategy and Management Outlook

National Beverage is committed to expanding its premium portfolio giving emphasis on sparkling water and zero sugar products.

While LaCroix remains the milestone brand, management is accelerating growth in complementary categories such as flavored sparkling waters and functional beverages targeting more specialized consumer audiences.

In recent results, management highlighted that consumer behavior remains cautious, especially in lower income groups.

FIZZ products, however, are also more durable as their consumer base is quality and lifestyle-centric.

The company, through this distinction, avoids aggressive price reductions and protect its profit margins.

Regarding innovation, the company keeps introducing new flavors and limited editions not with the aim of explosive volume growth but to maintain its brand engagement.

This strategy seems effective, supporting the loyalty of clients without significant cost increase.

At the same time the company's management is cautious to its estimates avoiding ambitious guidance for accelerated growth.

Instead, the message focuses on consistency, efficiency and long-term value creation.

The absence of acquisitions and aggressive investment may disappoint investors that are attracted by quick expansion but is aligned with the company's DNA.

Dividend Policy and Capital Returns

National Beverage is not a classic dividend pick, despite its long history of distributions.

The company does not follow a consistent or predictable regular dividend policy. It chooses instead to distribute special dividends when management believes that liquidity and conditions permit it.

The most recent example was the special dividend of $3.25 per share in 2024, while in the past there have been extraordinary payments including $6.00 in 2020 and $3.00 in 2021.

The lack of annual repeatability means that the investor cannot rely on a specific dividend yield as a fixed income element.

Instead, dividends work more as a mechanism for returning excess liquidity when the company does not see decent reinvestment opportunities.

While the balance sheet is strong and free of debt, FIZZ does is not for investors looking for reliable cash income. Dividends are better viewed as an added benefit rather than a foundational investment feature.

Valuation

With a current stock price of $35 and a P/E of 18x, National Beverage is valued at a market cap. of around $3.3 billion, taking into account the 93.62 million shares outstanding.

While this level is slightly below the historical norm, it does not suggest a compelling valuation for investors focused on growth.

In the base case scenario where P/E remain stable and the company continues to expand with low single digit pace, a P/E of 18x is considered fair.

In this case, the stock is trading around current levels, with limited upside but also controlled downside risk, given the strong balance sheet and the absence of debt.

In the bear case scenario competitive pressure intensifies margins are squeezed and growth slows even more, the market could value FIZZ at a P/E of 15x-16x.

With the same earnings, this would move the stock lower (to levels way below $30) reflecting a more conservative stance on the company's prospects. In the bull case scenario, a return to more substantial organic growth combined with margin improvement or new successful products, could justify a P/E of 20x-21x.

In this case, the stock could move towards $40, but without drastically changing its investment profile. Overall, the current valuation supports a hold recommendation, not an aggressive placement.

On NASDAQ, the stock shows this soft investment approach.

As we can see in the chart, the range of fluctuation is narrow.

The downward movement should be noted, although not as aggressive, with FIZZ trading near 6-year lows, implying investor indifference even at such low levels.

The long-term trend is located to $28 (-20%) with the upper level range extending toward $65.

Risks

All investments have their risks. National Beverage's main risk is limited volume growth.

In an industry where larger players invest aggressively in marketing and distribution, FIZZ’s more smooth approach could lead to gradual market share erosion over time.

The second risk relates to the company’s significant dependence on LaCroix. Although the brand continues to perform well, changes in consumer trends or increased competition in premium offerings could weigh more heavily on financial performance.

Diversification efforts are progressing but not fast enough to meaningfully mitigate this exposure.

Cost fluctuations in raw materials, packaging and transportation are source of uncertainty.

If costs rise again, margins could come under pressure, particularly if the company absorbs part of the increase.

Finally, the stock's valuation limits the margin for error. At levels above the historical average for the industry, any disappointing results or sign of a slowdown could lead to a sharp price adjustment.

Conclusion

National Beverage remains a high quality, profitable and financially strong company, supported by a clear strategy and consistent execution.

Recent results confirm the stability of its business model but do not point to an acceleration in growth.

At current valuation levels, the stock offers limited upside but provides a degree of downside protection.

Overall, the picture supports a Hold recommendation for FIZZ stock, with an emphasis on quality rather than rapid returns.
